title: Understanding AI Context & Tokens
description: Learn how tokens work, why context windows matter, and how to optimize your AI interactions for better performance and lower costs.

cards.title: Master AI Context & Tokens
cards.body: Ever wonder why AI sometimes gets expensive or forgets what you said? It all comes down to tokens and context windows. You'll learn how AI processes text, why it costs what it costs, and how to optimize your prompts for better results.

cards.title: What Are Tokens?
cards.body: Tokens are the fundamental units that AI models actually understand. They're not quite words — they can be whole words, parts of words, characters, or punctuation.

analogy.text: Think of tokens like puzzle pieces. AI doesn't see your sentence as words — it breaks everything into small numbered chunks that fit together to represent meaning.

bullets: One word might be multiple tokens
Short words are often single tokens
Punctuation usually gets its own token
Models work with numbers, not text

cards.title: See Tokens in Action
cards.body: Let's use OpenAI's tokenizer to see how text becomes tokens. This will show you exactly how your prompts get processed.

source.author: Ras Mic
source.description: Watch how text gets converted to tokens using OpenAI's tokenizer

bullets: Go to platform.openai.com/tokenizer
Paste any text you want to analyze
Watch how it breaks into colored token chunks
Note the character count vs token count

cards.title: Why Token Count Matters
cards.body: AI pricing is based on tokens, not words. Understanding this helps you save money and get faster responses.
bullets: Input tokens: what you send to the AI
Output tokens: what the AI sends back
Both input and output count toward your bill
Smaller prompts = cheaper + faster responses
cards.warn: Output tokens typically cost 5-10x more than input tokens, so asking for shorter responses can save significant money.

cards.title: Token Knowledge Check
cards.question: If a 280-character paragraph converts to 52 tokens, what's the approximate character-to-token ratio?

options.text: Tokens and characters are the same

cards.correctFeedback: Exactly! There's roughly 5-6 characters per token on average, though this varies by language and content type.
cards.wrongFeedback: Not quite. In this example, 280 characters became 52 tokens — that's about 5-6 characters per token.

cards.title: Understanding Context Windows
cards.body: The context window is like AI's short-term memory — it's the total amount of text (in tokens) the model can remember and reference in a conversation.

analogy.text: Imagine reading a book but only being able to remember the last 20 pages. That's what a context window is like for AI — it can only 'remember' a limited amount of recent conversation.

bullets: Different models have different context limits
Includes your messages + AI responses + system prompts
When full, older messages get 'forgotten'
Larger context = more expensive processing

cards.title: Check Your Context Usage
cards.body: If you're using Claude Code, you can see exactly how much context you're using and how much space is left.

source.author: Ras Mic
source.description: See how to check context usage in Claude Code

cards.codeCaption: This shows your token usage breakdown and remaining space
bullets: Shows system prompt token usage
Displays tools and skills token cost
Reveals how much free space remains
Warns when auto-compact will trigger

cards.title: Halfway There!
cards.body: You now understand what tokens are, how context windows work, and why they matter for cost and performance. Next up: how to optimize your prompts!

cards.title: When Context Gets Full
cards.body: When your context window approaches its limit, AI models start to struggle and hallucinate more. Understanding this helps you manage long conversations better.
bullets: Performance degrades near context limits
Auto-compact summarizes old messages
Some information gets lost in compression
Models work best at 50-60% context capacity
cards.warn: Stay under 50-60% of your context window for optimal performance. When you hit 80-90%, consider starting a fresh conversation.

cards.title: Optimize Your Prompts
cards.body: Now let's reduce your token footprint. Every token you save makes your AI interactions faster and cheaper.
bullets: Remove duplicate examples — one good example beats three similar ones
Cut boilerplate — skip 'please' and 'thank you' in prompts
Summarize long documents before pasting them
Use bullet points instead of full sentences when possible
cards.safe: Don't worry about being too brief — AI models prefer concise, clear instructions over polite conversation.

cards.title: Test Your Optimizations
cards.body: Put your new knowledge to work. Take a prompt you've used before and optimize it for fewer tokens.
bullets: Copy an old prompt you've used
Paste it into OpenAI's tokenizer
Remove unnecessary words and examples
Compare the before/after token counts

cards.title: Context Management Check
cards.question: What's the best practice for context window usage to maintain optimal AI performance?

options.text: Fill it completely for maximum context

cards.correctFeedback: Perfect! Staying under 50-60% context capacity helps maintain optimal performance and reduces hallucinations.
cards.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Models perform best when you stay under 50-60% of the context window capacity.

cards.title: Bonus: Safe Sandboxes with Daytona
cards.body: For AI agents that need to execute code safely, Daytona provides pre-warmed sandboxes with file systems, code execution, and LSP support.

source.author: Ras Mic
source.description: See how Daytona makes it easy to spin up safe development sandboxes

bullets: Initialize Daytona with simple SDK
Create sandbox and specify language
Get full file system access
Built-in LSP and code execution

cards.title: Context Master!
cards.body: You've mastered the fundamentals of AI context and tokens! You now know how to optimize prompts for cost and performance, understand context window limits, and can manage long AI conversations effectively.
